coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of lignin-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with excellent fluorescence stability, quantum yield, and biocompatibility for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) has remained a challenging endeavor. Using an acidolysis process with 17.5% nitric acid followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, this study provided an improved synthesis route for the production of high-quality GQDs from alkali lignin. The nitrogen-doped GQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence stability under a wide range of pH (3–10), duration (1–12 h), and [NaCl] (0–1000 mM) conditions, and have a high quantum yield of 28%. The GQDs or GQDs/Fe3+ sensing systems ([GQDs] at 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] at 500 μmol L−1, and UV excitation at 370 nm) for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ or AA have excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility. For Fe3+ and AA, the limit of detection is 1.49 and 1.62 μmol L−1, respectively. Mechanism investigation shows that photoluminescence quenching is caused by the formation of GQDs-Fe3+ complexes, whereas fluorescence recovery is due to Fe3+ reduction by AA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Two-step synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from alkali lignin.
Morphology and particle size distribution of GQDs: a) TEM image, b) HR-TEM image, c) AFM image, d) particle diameter, e) particle height, and f) BET isotherm with pore size distribution.

Characterization of crystalline and chemical structures: a) XRD spectra of GQDs and alkaline lignin (AL); b) Raman spectrum of GQDs, c) FT–IR spectra of GQDs and AL, d) XPS survey scans of GQDs and AL, e) AL–C 1s, f) AL–O 1s, g) GQDs–N 1s, h) GQDs–C 1s, and i) GQDs–O 1s.

Photoluminescence (PL) properties of GQD aqueous solution at 100 mg L−1: a) photos of cuvettes in the sun or exposed to UV radiation at 365 nm; b) UV–vis absorbance, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum with fixed emission at 443 nm, and PL spectrum with fixed excitation at 370 nm; c) PL spectra at excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm with a 10 nm increment; PL stability under different d) irradiation time, e) pH value, and f) NaCl concentration with UV excitation at 370 nm.
HeLa cell viability after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h or 48 h with different GQD concentrations.

Confocal images of HeLa cells: a) incubated with 200 mg L−1 GQDs for 4 h, b) followed by incubation with 300 μmol L−1 Fe3+ for 2 h, and c) further treated with 300 μmol L−1 AA for 1 h, under UV irradiation at 405 nm. Subplots d–f and g–i are bright-field images and merged images, respectively.

Application of GQDs in sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+: a) photoluminescence (PL) spectra at various Fe3+ concentrations (0– 400 μmol L−1, GQDs at 50 mg L−1, UV excitation at 370 nm). Inset images: left – fluorescence of the blank GQDs solution, right – effective fluorescence quenching at [Fe3+] of 400 μmol L−1; b) linear relationship between relative peak PL intensity at 453 nm (I 0/I) and [Fe3+]; c) relative loss of PL intensities – GQDs aqueous solutions with the presence of individual interference metal ions at 1000 μmol L−1 (bars in grey), or with the presence of both individual metal ions at 1000 μmol L−1 and Fe3+ ions at 500 μmol L−1 (bars in purple). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Application of the GQDs/Fe3+ system to sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid (AA): a) photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different AA concentrations (0– 300 μmol L−1, GQDs at 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] at 500 μmol L−1, UV excitation at 370 nm; inset image: left – fluorescence quenched with Fe3+ at 500 μmol L−1, right – partly recovered with the addition of AA; b) linear relationship between relative peak PL intensity at 453 nm (I 0/I) and [AA]; c) relative gain of PL intensities – GQDs/Fe3+ solution with the presence of individual positive control chemicals at 262.5 μmol L−1 (bars in grey), or with the presence of both individual positive control chemicals and AA at 262.5 μmol L−1 (bars in cyan). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Mechanisms underlying photoluminescence quenching and recovery: a) UV absorption spectra of the GQDs solution at 50 mg L−1, with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0– 400 μmol L−1); b) zeta potential of GQDs/Fe3+ and GQDs (inset image); c) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs/Fe3+ before and after adding AA, in the presence of 1,10-phenanthroline (C12H8N2); d) chemical reactions of the 1,10-phenanthroline method.
Synthesis of lignin-based nanomaterials and their application in fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GQDs-based fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) in lake water samples.

head.ce:title: Lignin-based fluorescence-switchable graphene quantum dots for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Comprehensive Laboratory of Forestry, College of Forestry, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Comprehensive Laboratory of Forestry, College of Forestry, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of lignin-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with excellent fluorescence stability, quantum yield, and biocompatibility for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) has remained a challenging endeavor. Using an acidolysis process with 17.5% nitric acid followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, this study provided an improved synthesis route for the production of high-quality GQDs from alkali lignin. The nitrogen-doped GQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence stability under a wide range of pH (3–10), duration (1–12 h), and [NaCl] (0–1000 mM) conditions, and have a high quantum yield of 28%. The GQDs or GQDs/Fe3+ sensing systems ([GQDs] at 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] at 500 μmol L−1, and UV excitation at 370 nm) for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ or AA have excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility. For Fe3+ and AA, the limit of detection is 1.49 and 1.62 μmol L−1, respectively. Mechanism investigation shows that photoluminescence quenching is caused by the formation of GQDs-Fe3+ complexes, whereas fluorescence recovery is due to Fe3+ reduction by AA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn much attention in recent years due to their excellent optoelectronic characteristics, availability of edge sites for functionalization, high solubility and stability in various solvents, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. GQDs have been proposed for use in a variety of applications such as fluorescence sensing [3–6], biological imaging and drug delivery [7,8], energy storage [9,10], and enhanced photocatalysis [11].

Bottom-up synthesis of GQDs has largely trumped top-down synthesis, with the latter being referred to as chemical, electrochemical, or hydrothermal exfoliation of costly precursors such as graphene sheets and carbon fibers [12,13]. Bottom-up synthesis of GQDs has traditionally used polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), coal, and other chemicals/materials as carbon sources [14–16], but these techniques often suffer from environmental drawbacks and undesirable properties of derived GQDs [17]. Eco-friendly precursors have gained in popularity in recent years, with lignin being the most notable. Alkali lignin, organosolv lignin, hydrolysis lignin, and pyrolytic lignin are the most important industrial lignins, with a large potential for boosting global supply [18]. Lignin is a renewable aromatic polymer with high carbon content, distinctive aromatic structures, and an abundance of functionalization edge sites, making it ideal for the production of high-quality GQDs [19,20].

Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) is important in environmental, biological, and diagnostic applications [21–23]. Lignin-based GQDs or similar nanomaterials have been synthesized and explored as fluorescent probes for Fe3+ and AA detection. A summary of recent research advances in this field is listed in Table 1 . Despite these efforts, the development and application of lignin-based GQDs for the detection of Fe3+ and AA remains in their early stages for three reasons: 1) fluorescence stability and quantum yield are not satisfactory; 2) cytotoxicity and cell imaging at higher GQD dosages have not been thoroughly investigated; and 3) the limit of detection (LOD) varies significantly between these research, necessitating additional in-depth sensitivity and selectivity studies.

The objective of this research is to develop lignin-based GQDs with sufficient sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ and AA detection. To that end, the synthesis route for lignin-based GQDs has been optimized to achieve excellent fluorescence stability, quantum yield, and biocompatibility. The sensitivity and selectivity of the GQDs (for Fe3+ detection) or GQDs/Fe3+ (for AA detection) fluorescence sensing systems are thoroughly investigated and validated using real-world water samples. The mechanisms of photoluminescence quenching and recovery are also studied.

Alkali lignin (AL) with an average molecular weight of about 10,000 g mol−1 and a low sulfonate content (4%, pH 10.5) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate (≥99%) and nitric acid (70%) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3, FeCl2, NaCl, KCl, AgNO3, CuCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2, ZnCl2, and AlCl3 (≥99%) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). KH2PO4-Na2HPO4 buffer solution (0.2 mol L−1, pH 6.86) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), L-glutamic acid (Glu), L-tyrosine (Tyr), lysine (Lys), phenylalanine (Phe), methionine (Met), homocysteine (Hcy), glycine (Gly), and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). All chemicals were used as received, with no further purification. Dialysis membranes giving a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3000 Da were manufactured by Viskase® Companies, Inc. (Lombard, IL, USA). Microporous membranes with a pore size of 0.22 μm and a diameter of 50 mm were produced by Xinya Purification Technologies Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was prepared by a YL-200B water supply system (Yiliyuan Water Treatment Equipment Ltd., Shenzhen, China) and used throughout the experiments.

A two-step scheme was proposed and optimized for the synthesis of GQDs using alkali lignin as the carbon source (Fig. 1 ). The first step is to fragment polymeric lignin using refluxing nitric acid, resulting in a large amount of nitrated lignin intermediates for subsequent hydrothermal treatment. The second step involves aromatic refusion under hydrothermal treatment with an alkaline aqueous solution, where the hydroxyl groups play a crucial role in controlling the particle size, edge functionalization, and optical properties of GQDs.

Alkali lignin (3 g) was dissolved in a mixture of 10 mL of 70% HNO3 and 30 mL of H2O (or 40 mL of 17.5% HNO3 aqueous solution). Lignin acidolysis was carried out at 80 °C for 8 h with continuous stirring and liquid refluxing. The resultant suspension was cooled down to room temperature before being centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to separate a solid and a liquid (recovered nitric acid). The solid intermediates were dispersed in 30 mL of NaOH solution at 0.2 mol L−1, by applying ultrasonication at 40 kHz and 500 W for 1 h. The mixture was then placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting yellowish slurry was first filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble carbon particles. Following that, the supernatant was placed into a dialysis bag and immersed in deionized water for 48 h to eliminate minor contaminants and achieve a pH of around 7. After 24 h of freeze-drying, purified GQDs were obtained.

The morphology of GQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos™ F200s, FEI company, OR, USA) at 80 kV, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Tecnai™ G2 F20, FEI company, OR, USA) at 200 kV, and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nano Man VS, Bruker Corporation, MA, USA). The particle size distribution of the GQDs, including diameter and height (thickness), was determined statistically on over 100 dots of the TEM and AFM images using the Nano Measurer software (v1.2, developed by the Department of Chemistry, Fudan University, Shanghai, China). The surface area and porosity of the GQDs were evaluated by BET analysis (ASAP 2460 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer, Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, GA, USA), using N2 adsorption and desorption at the bath temperature of 77.3 K.

For crystallinity, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were acquired with a Bruker D8 (Bruker Corporation, MA, USA), using Cu Kα radiation at generator voltage and current of 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively. The Raman spectrum was given by a Renishaw 2000 Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw plc, Gloucestershire, UK) equipped with a 532 nm laser beam. The functional groups of GQDs were identified by a Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectrometer (Nicolet™ iS™10 with a resolution of 4 cm−1, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab™ 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA). Elemental mapping images of C, O, and N were acquired by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) using an Oxford X-Max 20 system (Oxford Instruments plc, Abingdon, UK).

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to examine the photoluminescence capabilities of the GQDs, and a UV-2450 spectrometer was used to obtain the photoluminescence absorption spectrum (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan).

The fluorescence quantum yield (Ф) of GQDs, i.e., the ratio of photons emitted vs. absorbed, was determined using the techniques described by Shen et al. [33]. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 of H2SO4 was chosen as the standard, giving a reference quantum yield (Ф R) of 54%. To minimize the inner filter effect, the GQDs samples were properly diluted to have UV absorbance values of 0.05–0.1 [34]. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs was calculated using the following formula:
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
where I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density, and n is the refractive index. The subscript R denotes the reference sample of quinine sulfate.

In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs was assayed by using the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8, Sigma-Aldrich) and HeLa cells [35]. First, HeLa cells were proliferated in Corning® Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). Then, 100 μL of cells were seeded into the 96-well tissue culture plates filled with DMEM, at the density of 4 × 103 cells per well. Incubation was carried out in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2, at 37 °C for 24 h. Following incubation, the cells were treated with GQDs of different concentrations, i.e., 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 125, 150, 200, 250, or 300 mg L−1, for 24 or 48 h. Subsequently, the cells were rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) three times before 100 μL of CCK-8 solution was added into each well. Incubation was continued for another 4 h, using the same conditions as before. After incubation, optical density was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm using a Spark™ 10 M microplate reader (Tecan Group Ltd., Männedorf, Switzerland), and the percentage of cell viability was calculated. The trials were done in triplicate, and the average was obtained.

For cell imaging, each well of the confocal dish was seeded with cultured HeLa cells, which were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 environment. A GQD aqueous solution at 200 mg L−1 was added into each well of the confocal dish, and the incubation period was extended for another 4 h. To test the feasibility of using GQDs to detect Fe3+ and AA in living HeLa cells, Fe3+ at 300 μmol L−1 was introduced into the living cell system, and after 2 h, AA at 300 μmol L−1 was added before further incubation of 1 h. The culture medium was then withdrawn, and the cells were thoroughly washed with the PBS solution. Cell fluorescence imaging was taken using an FV1200 laser scanning microscope (Olympus Corporation, Tokyo, Japan), at the wavelength of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: To detect Fe3+, a 100 mg L−1 aqueous solution of GQDs was prepared. Certain amounts of anhydrous ferric chloride were dissolved in the KH2PO4-Na2HPO4 buffer solution (0.2 mol L−1, pH 6.86) to derive a series of solutions with Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 1000 μmol L−1. Aqueous solutions containing individual interference irons, i.e., Na+, K+, Ag+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Al3+, were prepared at a concentration of 2000 μmol L−1. For sensitivity tests, the GQDs solution was blended with the FeCl3 solutions at a volumetric ratio of 1:1. After thorough mixing, the resulting solutions had [GQDs] of 50 mg L−1 and various [Fe3+] of up to 500 μmol L−1. Their photoluminescence emission spectra were subsequently recorded using a 370 nm excitation wavelength. The peak photoluminescence intensities at different Fe3+ concentrations are plotted to give a linear relationship. For selectivity tests, solutions of potential interference metal ions were blended with the GQDs aqueous suspension at a volumetric ratio of 1:1. The resulting mixtures had [GQDs] of 50 mg L−1 and individual metal ions of 1000 μmol L−1. Likewise, the photoluminescence emission spectra and their peak intensities were taken. All these tests were performed in triplicate, with average results and standard errors recorded.

To detect AA, a fluorescence-switchable sensing system was first produced using a mixture solution of GQDs at 100 mg L−1 and FeCl3 at 1000 μmol L−1. A series of AA solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 600 μmol L−1 were prepared. Interference compounds such as Glu, Tyr, Lys, Phe, Met, Hcy, Gly, and CA were examined. Solutions of individual interference chemicals at a concentration of 525 μmol L−1 were also prepared. For sensitivity tests, the GQDs/Fe3+ solution was blended with AA solutions at a volumetric ratio of 1:1. The resulting solutions included [GQDs] of 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] of 500 μmol L−1, and a range of [AA] of up to 300 μmol L−1. For selectivity tests, the GQDs/Fe3+ solution was blended with solutions of potential interference chemicals at the same volumetric ratio of 1:1, giving a concentration of interference chemicals at 262.5 μmol L−1. The photoluminescence emission spectra and their peak intensities were subsequently recorded. Experiments were also carried out in triplicate.

To validate the applicability of the GQDs-based fluorescence sensing system for Fe3+ and AA monitoring in complex, real-life samples, water samples from the “West Lake” at the Northwest A&F University were tested. The water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through 0.22 μM membranes before adding different amounts of FeCl3 and AA. These samples were mixed with the GQDs or GQDs/Fe3+ sensing systems (GQDs at 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] at 500 μmol L−1), and photoluminescence spectra with UV excitation at 370 nm were obtained.
The zeta potential of the GQDs or GQDs/Fe3+ solution was measured by a Mastersizer 2000 instrument (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK).

The morphology and nanoparticle size distribution of GQDs were studied by TEM, HR-TEM, AFM, and BET analyses. As shown in Fig. 2a, the GQDs were quasi-cylindrical and evenly dispersed in the aqueous solution. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 2b) illustrates that the GQDs have crystalline structures with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, characterized by the presence of the graphite (1100) crystal plane [36]. The AFM image (Fig. 2c) depicts the three-dimensional topography of the GQD surface in greater detail. The particles have a diameter ranging from 1 to 6 nm (Fig. 2d), according to the statistical analysis of the TEM image. Most GQDs have a height (or thickness) of 0.3 to 1.0 nm (Fig. 2e), indicating that they are composed of only a few atomic layers [32]. The BET surface area and the average pore diameter of adsorption were determined to be 65.98 m2/g and 1.65 nm, respectively.

The crystalline characteristics of GQDs were studied with XRD and Raman spectroscopy. According to the XRD spectrum shown in Fig. 3a, the GQDs have a distinctive graphite (002) peak located at 2θ = 26.4° (JCPDS #41-1487). The interlayer spacing is 3.40 Å, which is the same as in bulk graphite [37,38]. In comparison to AL, which has a weak peak at 2θ = 22.1°, the shift to the right and enrichment of the peak indicate conversion from amorphous AL to single-crystalline GQDs [32]. Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectroscopy of GQDs, where the D band at 1353 cm−1 is associated with the vibrations of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and can be regarded as the disordered band. The strong peak in the G band at around 1568 cm−1 represents the in-plane vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and is typical of the well-structured graphite single crystals [39,40]. The ratio of relative intensities of the disorder-induced D peak and the G peak (I D/I G) is 0.95, indicating that the GQDs are structurally comparable to graphite [41]. Furthermore, a broad peak in the 2D band emerges at 2776 cm−1, which is proportional to the number of graphene layers [13]. The graphene structure of a few layers is consistent with the AFM findings shown in Fig. 2c and e.

The chemical structures of the GQDs and AL were studied using FT–IR, with their spectra presented in Fig. 3c. The broad peak at 3443 cm−1 is attributed to OH of the aliphatic and phenolic structures. The strong peak at 1590 cm−1 caused by CC telescopic vibration indicates the presence of a significant number of sp2 structures [42]. This backs up the HR-TEM observations in Fig. 2b. Aromatic CH bonds are responsible for the peaks at 1049 cm−1 and 827 cm−1. These findings show that the basic structure of the lignin benzene ring is retained following hydrothermal treatment for aromatic refusion. When compared to the AL feedstock, the GQDs have a substantially lower absorbance peak at 1190 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C − O − C) and a stronger peak at 1660 cm−1 (C=O), indicating that the ether bonds are broken and more oxygen-containing functional groups are formed [43]. The GQDs are rich in polar functional groups, e.g., O−H, CO, and C−O, implying excellent solubility and stability in polar solvents. Indeed, the aqueous GQDs solution at a concentration of 2.0 g L−1 remained stable for at least 6 months with no precipitation or color change (Fig. S1). It should be noted that the presence of a C−N peak at 1415 cm−1 indicates that the GQDs were nitrogen-doped.

XPS spectra were acquired to better understand the change of chemical bonds from AL to GQDs. The survey scans (Fig. 3d) include C 1s (284.5 eV) and O 1s (531.5 eV) peaks for both GQDs and AL, as well as an additional N peak (399 eV) for GQDs due to nitrogen doping. Nitrogen was introduced during lignin acidolysis with nitric acid, which is supported by the XPS spectra of nitrated-AL (Fig. S2). Fig. 3e–f, h–i show high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra to assist explain the bond configurations of C and O atoms in the GQDs and AL. The C 1s spectrum is split into four peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to C−C/C=C, C−O, C−N, and CO, respectively [44]. Except for the newly formed CN bond, the intensity of CO in GQDs is substantially lower than that of AL, demonstrating that ether cleavage happened and confirming the FT–IR study results. Meanwhile, the intensity of CO in GQDs has considerably risen (see Table S1 for details). When nitric acid cleaves the majority of the ether bonds in AL, oxidation groups are formed [45]. The CN bond in GQDs can be further explained in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3g). Deconvolution of the N 1s peak yields C–N–C signals at 399.1 eV, N–(C)3 signals at 400 eV, and NH signals at 400.9 eV [27], with atomic percentages of 29.02%, 43.39%, and 27.59%, respectively. Based on these findings, it is possible to conclude that nitric acid assists in the breakdown of the β-O-4 bonds during acidolysis and that nitrogen doping occurs in the basal plane and at the edges of GQDs. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy confirmed that the N element is spread uniformly in the GQDs (Fig. S3).

When subjected to UV irradiation at 365 nm, the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs at 100 mg L−1 changes from light brown to dazzling blue (Fig. 4a). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs solution shows an absorbance peak at ~257 nm and a weak shoulder peak at ~340 nm (Fig. 4b). These two absorbance peaks correspond to the π–π* transitions of CC and the n–π* transition between sp2 aromatic domains and oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups, respectively [46,47]. The former absorbance peak can be found in the raw material of AL (Fig. S4), albeit at a shorter wavelength of 229 nm. This implies that GQDs have higher integrity of π-conjugated carbons and can induce the π–π* transition with lower photon energy, which is consistent with a previous report [48]. According to Fig. 4b, the maximum photoluminescence emission occurs at 453 nm at the ideal excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

Full photoluminescence emission spectra of the GQDs aqueous solution, at different excitation wavelengths, are shown in Fig. 4c. When the excitation wavelength varies from 300 to 500 nm in increments of 10 nm, the corresponding main photoluminescence peaks show a redshift from 410 to 515 nm. Its peak intensity rises initially and then falls, with a maximum at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. It is not uncommon for the emission wavelength and intensity in carbon-based fluorescent materials to be substantially excitation-dependent [27,32,49]. This phenomenon is mostly caused by particle size distribution or emissive states arising from various functional groups [50].

Excellent photoluminescence stability is sought in fluorescence-based applications. The fluorescence stability of the GQDs solution was examined for this purpose under UV irradiation for an extended period of time, as well as when subjected to different pH values and NaCl concentrations. Under continuous UV irradiation at 365 nm with a 30 W lamp for 12 h, the GQDs solution maintains very strong photostability (Fig. 4d). After 12 h, just 3% of the photoluminescence intensity was lost. Furthermore, the GQDs solution has excellent photoluminescence stability at pH values ranging from 3 to 10 (Fig. 4e) or at high NaCl concentrations up to 1 mol L−1 (Fig. 4f).

The fluorescence quantum yield (Ф) was calculated to be 28%, which is higher than previously reported values for lignin-based nanomaterials listed in Table 1. Zhu et al. [51] identified three factors that improve quantum yield: augmentation of photoluminescence centers via edge modification, enrichment of structure electron density via reduction, and suppression of non-radiative recombination. An in-depth analysis is necessary to establish the rationale for the high quantum yield of the GQDs synthesized in this study.

The cytotoxicity of GQDs was examined using model HeLa cells. Cell survival percentages at various GQD dosages are shown in Fig. 5 . According to statistical analysis, when the GQD concentration is less than 50 mg L−1, there is no toxicity on the HeLa cells for 24 or 48 h when compared to the blank control. Even at higher GQD concentrations of up to 300 mg L−1, the cells maintain more than 80% viability. This is better than or comparable to previous research findings (Table 1). Because of their reduced cytotoxicity, the GQDs developed in this study may be more friendly to delicate or fragile cells.

Based on their superior photoluminescence properties and low cytotoxicity, the applicability of GQDs in cellular imaging was assessed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. HeLa cells were incubated for 4 h with a 200 mg L−1 GQDs solution before confocal microscopic images were captured (Fig. 6a). The cells impregnated with GQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence, indicating that the GQDs can be internalized into the cells and retain their fluorescence characteristics. The fluorescence of GQDs is localized in the cytoplasm, with very faint emission signals detected in the nucleus, which is consistent with previous studies [33,43]. These findings demonstrate that the nano-sized GQDs can easily penetrate living cells without accessing the nucleus. After pretreatment with GQDs/Fe3+ ([Fe3+] at 300 μmol L−1), the fluorescence of HeLa cells became very dim (Fig. 6b). Further, the addition of AA at a concentration of 300 μmol L−1 would restore the majority of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6c). Meanwhile, morphological studies show that GQD administration has no significant effect on the HeLa cells (Fig. 6d–f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Excellent fluorescence stability (under different pH, time, and [NaCl] conditions), a high quantum yield of 28%, minimal cytotoxicity, and the “on−off−on” fluorescence response with Fe3+and AA suggest the possibility of using GQDs for fluorescence sensing in environmental or physiological scenarios, provided that the sensitivity and selectivity are high enough.

The photoluminescence spectra of the GQD solution added with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, under UV excitation at 370 nm, are presented in Fig. 7a. Peak photoluminescence intensities occur at the wavelength of 453 nm. At higher [Fe3+], the photoluminescence intensity steadily diminishes. When the concentration of Fe3+ reaches 400 μmol L−1, only 32.7% of the initial fluorescence intensity persists. The linear relationship between the relative peak intensity of photoluminescence (I 0/I) and [Fe3+] is given in Fig. 7b, with a slope of 0.0050. A correlation coefficient of 0.9996 indicates excellent linearity. The lower LOD was calculated to be 1.49 μmol L−1, according to the formula of LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The GQDs have a reasonable LOD for Fe3+ sensing in drinking water as it is lower than the maximum permitted limit (0.3 ppm or 5.37 μmol L−1) imposed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [52]. Similarly, the linearity was investigated at various GQD dosages, i.e., 50, 75, 100, and 150 mg L−1. Regardless of the GQDs concentration used, excellent linearity and extremely comparable slopes are found (Fig. S5). This demonstrates that the detection of Fe3+ ions is independent of the concentration of GQDs.

Fig. 7c shows the relative loss of photoluminescence intensities (1-I/I 0) when interference metal ions are present in the test samples, at a high concentration of 1000 μmol L−1. The presence of K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, or Cd2+ has minimal impact on Fe3+ detection, but Ni2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Na+, Ag+, and Cu2+ may account for up to 5% of fluorescence loss (purple bars in Fig. 7c). Given the high concentrations of interference ions used in these tests, it is reasonable to assume that the GQDs have a pretty high selectivity for Fe3+ detection. Even when other metal ions are present in the test sample, the GQDs can successfully detect Fe3+ ions.

The detection of Fe3+ ions is based on fluorescence quenching, i.e., the “turn-off” response induced by Fe3+. Fluorescence-quenched GQDs/Fe3+, on the other hand, can be switched “on” by raising the concentration of AA (see inset of Fig. 8a). The GQDs/Fe3+ system may be used to detect AA based on this concept. As shown in Fig. 8a, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs/Fe3+ system increases linearly with AA concentrations ranging from 0 to 300 μmol L−1, whereas the spectra shape and position are quite similar. When [AA] approaches 300 μmol L−1, the fluorescence quenched by the presence of Fe3+ may be promptly restored and increased by 2.14-fold (Fig. 8b). The slope of the linear relationship between fluorescence gain (I/I 0–1) and AA concentration is 0.0038, where I and I 0 are the photoluminescence intensities of GQDs/Fe3+ with or without the presence of AA. Superior linearity is observed (R 2 = 0.9998). The limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/S) is as low as 1.62 μmol L−1.

To assess the selectivity of AA detection, fluorescence responses of the GQDs/Fe3+ system in the presence of positive control chemicals, including Glu, Tyr, Lys, Phe, Met, Hcy, Gly, and CA, were recorded and shown in Fig. 8c. At a high concentration of 262.5 μmol L−1, Glu, Tyr, and CA barely interfere with AA detection. When Gly and Hcy coexist in the test sample, however, they may contribute up to 25% gain in photoluminescence intensity (Fig. 8c, bars in cyan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility of the GQDs or GQDs/Fe3+ sensing systems ([GQDs] at 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] at 500 μmol L−1, and UV excitation at 370 nm) for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ or AA were further evaluated by testing lake water samples with spiked concentrations of Fe3+ or AA. Table 2 shows the test results. The GQDs sensing system achieves a decent recovery of 91.10%–100.92% at different Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 2 to 30 μmol L−1. The GQDs/Fe3+ sensing system provides a good recovery of 95.43%–101.19% for AA testing with concentrations ranging from 5 to 20 μmol L−1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of both sensing systems is as low as 0.71%. Despite interference from numerous unknown minerals and organic compounds in the lake water, the GQDs-based fluorescence sensing systems perform well in terms of reproducibility and accuracy, confirming the photoluminescence stability of the GQDs as well as sensitivity and selectivity of the sensing systems.

The GQDs-based sensing systems for Fe3+ and AA detection take advantage of the fluorescence “on–off–on” characteristics. The mechanisms underlying blue emission of GQDs have been extensively studied, with the hybridization structure of edge functional groups and intrinsic states in the highly crystalline structure being primarily responsible [51,53].

Photoluminescence quenching occurs when the GQDs–Fe3+ complex is formed. When [Fe3+] in the GQDs solution increases from 0 to 400 μmol L−1, the highest UV absorbance peak blue-shifts from 340 nm to 290 nm, with its amplitude increasing by 5 times (Fig. 9a). According to Liu et al. [54], this manifests as the formation of the GQDs–Fe3+ complex. This is supported further by the change in zeta potential. The zeta potential of GQDs is −7.6 mV, but it becomes +6.08 mV in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 9b). Complexes formed by chelating Fe3+ ions with functional groups of GQDs enhance charge transfer and fluorescence quenching [55]. It should be noted that Fe3+ ions absorb strongly at wavelengths between 300 and 350 nm (Fig. S6), whereas the GQDs need excitation at wavelengths ranging from 300 to 420 nm (centered at 370 nm, Fig. 4b). These two regions partially overlap, potentially resulting in an inner filter effect and a reduction in the limit of detection [56].

Photoluminescence recovery occurs when AA is introduced into the GQDs/Fe3+ sensing system and reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+. This is demonstrated by comparing the solutions before and after the addition of AA into the GQDs/Fe3+ system. The presence of Fe2+ was detected using the 1, 10-phenanthroline method (ASTM E394-15). In the presence of 1, 10-phenanthroline, an orange color was developed after adding AA into the GQDs/Fe3+ system (Fig. 9c). An absorbance peak near 510 nm characterizes the formation of [Fe(C8H12N2)3]2+ (Fig. 9c-d). The redox process causes the GQDs-Fe3+ complex to dissociate and the GQDs to revert to the normal conditions, restoring their photoluminescence. Other compounds that do not cause reduction of Fe3+ will not interfere with AA's ability to restore fluorescence. As a result, the GQDs/Fe3+ sensing system can be used as a “turn-on” fluorescence system for AA detection. It should be noted that in the absence of Fe3+, AA has no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (Fig. S7).

This study demonstrated the efficacy of an improved synthesis route for producing high-quality GQDs from alkali lignin. The nitrogen-doped GQDs were thoroughly characterized for their surface and physicochemical properties, photoluminescence properties, and cytotoxicity and cell imaging. The GQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence stability under a wide range of pH (3–10), duration (1–12 h), and [NaCl] (0–1000 mM) conditions, and have a high quantum yield of 28%. Real-world sample assay shows that the GQDs or GQDs/Fe3+ sensing systems ([GQDs] at 50 mg L−1, [Fe3+] at 500 μmol L−1, and UV excitation at 370 nm) for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ or AA have excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility. For Fe3+ and AA, the limit of detection is 1.49 and 1.62 μmol L−1, respectively. Mechanism investigation shows that photoluminescence quenching is caused by the formation of GQDs-Fe3+ complexes, whereas fluorescence recovery is due to Fe3+ reduction by AA.

Lingyan Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Dongbing Li: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - reviewing and editing. Heng Lu: Supervision, Resources. Shangkun Zhang: Investigation. Hao Gao: Visualization.
